It is discussed in further\\n91:  * detail below.\\n92:  *\\n93:  * <h3><a name=\\\"consumergroups\\\">Consumer Groups and Topic Subscriptions</a></h3>\\n94:  *\\n95:  * Kafka uses the concept of <i>consumer groups</i> to allow a pool of processes to divide the work of consuming and\\n96:  * processing records. These processes can either be running on the same machine or they can be\\n97:  * distributed over many machines to provide scalability and fault tolerance for processing. All consumer instances\\n98:  * sharing the same {@code group.id} will be part of the same consumer group.\\n99:  * <p>\\n100:  * Each consumer in a group can dynamically set the list of topics it wants to subscribe to through one of the\\n101:  * {@link #subscribe(Collection, ConsumerRebalanceListener) subscribe} APIs. Kafka will deliver each message in the\\n102:  * subscribed topics to one process in each consumer group. This is achieved by balancing the partitions between all\\n103:  * members in the consumer group so that each partition is assigned to exactly one consumer in the group. So if there\\n104:  * is a topic with four partitions, and a consumer group with two processes, each process would consume from two partitions.\\n105:  * <p>\\n106:  * Membership in a consumer group is maintained dynamically: if a process fails, the partitions assigned to it will\\n107:  * be reassigned to other consumers in the same group. Similarly, if a new consumer joins the group, partitions will be moved\\n108:  * from existing consumers to the new one. This is known as <i>rebalancing</i> the group and is discussed in more\\n109:  * detail <a href=\\\"#failuredetection\\\">below</a>. Group rebalancing is also used when new partitions are added\\n110:  * to one of the subscribed topics or when a new topic matching a {@link #subscribe(Pattern, ConsumerRebalanceListener) subscribed regex}\\n111:  * is created. The group will automatically detect the new partitions through periodic metadata refreshes and\\n112:  * assign them to members of the group.\\n113:  * <p>\\n114:  * Conceptually you can think of a consumer group as being a single logical subscriber that happens to be made up of\\n115:  * multiple processes. As a multi-subscriber system, Kafka naturally supports having any number of consumer groups for a\\n116:  * given topic without duplicating data (additional consumers are actually quite cheap).\\n117:  * <p>\\n118:  * This is a slight generalization of the functionality that is common in messaging systems. To get semantics similar to\\n119:  * a queue in a traditional messaging system all processes would be part of a single consumer group and hence record\\n120:  * delivery would be balanced over the group like with a queue. Unlike a traditional messaging system, though, you can\\n121:  * have multiple such groups. To get semantics similar to pub-sub in a traditional messaging system each process would\\n122:  * have its own consumer group, so each process would subscribe to all the records published to the topic.\\n123:  * <p>\\n124:  * In addition, when group reassignment happens automatically, consumers can be notified through a {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener},\\n125:  * which allows them to finish necessary application-level logic such as state cleanup, manual offset\\n126:  * commits, etc. See <a href=\\\"#rebalancecallback\\\">Storing Offsets Outside Kafka</a> for more details.\\n127:  * <p>\\n128:  * It is also possible for the consumer to <a href=\\\"#manualassignment\\\">manually assign</a> specific partitions\\n129:  * (similar to the older \\\"simple\\\" consumer) using {@link #assign(Collection)}. In this case, dynamic partition\\n130:  * assignment and consumer group coordination will be disabled.\\n131:  *\\n132:  * <h3><a name=\\\"failuredetection\\\">Detecting Consumer Failures</a></h3>\\n133:  *\\n134:  * After subscribing to a set of topics, the consumer will automatically join the group when {@link #poll(Duration)} is\\n135:  * invoked. The poll API is designed to ensure consumer liveness. As long as you continue to call poll, the consumer\\n136:  * will stay in the group and continue to receive messages from the partitions it was assigned. Underneath the covers,\\n137:  * the consumer sends periodic heartbeats to the server. If the consumer crashes or is unable to send heartbeats for\\n138:  * a duration of {@code session.timeout.ms}, then the consumer will be considered dead and its partitions will\\n139:  * be reassigned.\\n140:  * <p>\\n141:  * It is also possible that the consumer could encounter a \\\"livelock\\\" situation where it is continuing\\n142:  * to send heartbeats, but no progress is being made. To prevent the consumer from holding onto its partitions\\n143:  * indefinitely in this case, we provide a liveness detection mechanism using the {@code max.poll.interval.ms}\\n144:  * setting. Basically if you don't call poll at least as frequently as the configured max interval,\\n145:  * then the client will proactively leave the group so that another consumer can take over its partitions. When this happens,\\n146:  * you may see an offset commit failure (as indicated by a {@link CommitFailedException} thrown from a call to {@link #commitSync()}).\\n147:  * This is a safety mechanism which guarantees that only active members of the group are able to commit offsets.\\n148:  * So to stay in the group, you must continue to call poll.\\n149:  * <p>\\n150:  * The consumer provides two configuration settings to control the behavior of the poll loop:\\n151:  * <ol>\\n152:  *     <li><code>max.poll.interval.ms</code>: By increasing the interval between expected polls, you can give\\n153:  *     the consumer more time to handle a batch of records returned from {@link #poll(Duration)}. The drawback\\n154:  *     is that increasing this value may delay a group rebalance since the consumer will only join the rebalance\\n155:  *     inside the call to poll. You can use this setting to bound the time to finish a rebalance, but\\n156:  *     you risk slower progress if the consumer cannot actually call {@link #poll(Duration) poll} often enough.</li>\\n157:  *     <li><code>max.poll.records</code>: Use this setting to limit the total records returned from a single\\n158:  *     call to poll. This can make it easier to predict the maximum that must be handled within each poll\\n159:  *     interval. By tuning this value, you may be able to reduce the poll interval, which will reduce the\\n160:  *     impact of group rebalancing.</li>\\n161:  * </ol>\\n162:  * <p>\\n163:  * For use cases where message processing time varies unpredictably, neither of these options may be sufficient.\\n164:  * The recommended way to handle these cases is to move message processing to another thread, which allows\\n165:  * the consumer to continue calling {@link #poll(Duration) poll} while the processor is still working.\\n166:  * Some care must be taken to ensure that committed offsets do not get ahead of the actual position.\\n167:  * Typically, you must disable automatic commits and manually commit processed offsets for records only after the\\n168:  * thread has finished handling them (depending on the delivery semantics you need).\\n169:  * Note also that you will need to {@link #pause(Collection) pause} the partition so that no new records are received\\n170:  * from poll until after thread has finished handling those previously returned.\\n171:  *\\n172:  * <h3>Usage Examples</h3>\\n173:  * The consumer APIs offer flexibility to cover a variety of consumption use cases. Here are some examples to\\n174:  * demonstrate how to use them.\\n175:  *\\n176:  * <h4>Automatic Offset Committing</h4>\\n177:  * This example demonstrates a simple usage of Kafka's consumer api that relies on automatic offset committing.\\n178:  * <p>\\n179:  * <pre>\\n180:  * {@code\\n181:  *     Properties props = new Properties();\\n182:  *     props.setProperty(\\\"bootstrap.servers\\\", \\\"localhost:9092\\\");\\n183:  *     props.setProperty(\\\"group.id\\\", \\\"test\\\");\\n184:  *     props.setProperty(\\\"enable.auto.commit\\\", \\\"true\\\");\\n185:  *     props.setProperty(\\\"auto.commit.interval.ms\\\", \\\"1000\\\");\\n186:  *     props.setProperty(\\\"key.deserializer\\\", \\\"org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.StringDeserializer\\\");\\n187:  *     props.setProperty(\\\"value.deserializer\\\", \\\"org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.StringDeserializer\\\");\\n188:  *     KafkaConsumer<String, String> consumer = new KafkaConsumer<>(props);\\n189:  *     consumer.subscribe(Arrays.asList(\\\"foo\\\", \\\"bar\\\"));\\n190:  *     while (true) {\\n191:  *         ConsumerRecords<String, String> records = consumer.poll(Duration.ofMillis(100));\\n192:  *         for (ConsumerRecord<String, String> record : records)\\n193:  *             System.out.printf(\\\"offset = %d, key = %s, value = %s%n\\\", record.offset(), record.key(), record.value());\\n194:  *     }\\n195:  * }</pre>\\n196:  *\\n197:  * The connection to the cluster is bootstrapped by specifying a list of one or more brokers to contact using the\\n198:  * configuration {@code bootstrap.servers}. This list is just used to discover the rest of the brokers in the\\n199:  * cluster and need not be an exhaustive list of servers in the cluster (though you may want to specify more than one in\\n200:  * case there are servers down when the client is connecting).\\n201: \",\"url\":\"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-benchmarks/kafka--e678b4b@47ac12bd6042edf23f1dbca3d789db945d62802d/-/blob/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/clients/consumer/KafkaConsumer.java%3FL1-200\"}"
26. mcp__sourcegraph__sg_read_file @ -
Input
{
  "endLine": 400,
  "path": "clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/clients/consumer/KafkaConsumer.java",
  "repo": "github.com/sg-benchmarks/kafka--e678b4b",
  "startLine": 200
}
Output
"{\"content\":\"200:  * case there are servers down when the client is connecting).\\n201:  * <p>\\n202:  * Setting {@code enable.auto.commit} means that offsets are committed automatically with a frequency controlled by\\n203:  * the config {@code auto.commit.interval.ms}.\\n204:  * <p>\\n205:  * In this example the consumer is subscribing to the topics <i>foo</i> and <i>bar</i> as part of a group of consumers\\n206:  * called <i>test</i> as configured with {@code group.id}.\\n207:  * <p>\\n208:  * The deserializer settings specify how to turn bytes into objects. For example, by specifying string deserializers, we\\n209:  * are saying that our record's key and value will just be simple strings.\\n210:  *\\n211:  * <h4>Manual Offset Control</h4>\\n212:  *\\n213:  * Instead of relying on the consumer to periodically commit consumed offsets, users can also control when records\\n214:  * should be considered as consumed and hence commit their offsets. This is useful when the consumption of the messages\\n215:  * is coupled with some processing logic and hence a message should not be considered as consumed until it is completed processing.\\n216: \\n217:  * <p>\\n218:  * <pre>\\n219:  * {@code\\n220:  *     Properties props = new Properties();\\n221:  *     props.setProperty(\\\"bootstrap.servers\\\", \\\"localhost:9092\\\");\\n222:  *     props.setProperty(\\\"group.id\\\", \\\"test\\\");\\n223:  *     props.setProperty(\\\"enable.auto.commit\\\", \\\"false\\\");\\n224:  *     props.setProperty(\\\"key.deserializer\\\", \\\"org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.StringDeserializer\\\");\\n225:  *     props.setProperty(\\\"value.deserializer\\\", \\\"org.apache.kafka.common.serialization.StringDeserializer\\\");\\n226:  *     KafkaConsumer<String, String> consumer = new KafkaConsumer<>(props);\\n227:  *     consumer.subscribe(Arrays.asList(\\\"foo\\\", \\\"bar\\\"));\\n228:  *     final int minBatchSize = 200;\\n229:  *     List<ConsumerRecord<String, String>> buffer = new ArrayList<>();\\n230:  *     while (true) {\\n231:  *         ConsumerRecords<String, String> records = consumer.poll(Duration.ofMillis(100));\\n232:  *         for (ConsumerRecord<String, String> record : records) {\\n233:  *             buffer.add(record);\\n234:  *         }\\n235:  *         if (buffer.size() >= minBatchSize) {\\n236:  *             insertIntoDb(buffer);\\n237:  *             consumer.commitSync();\\n238:  *             buffer.clear();\\n239:  *         }\\n240:  *     }\\n241:  * }</pre>\\n242:  *\\n243:  * In this example we will consume a batch of records and batch them up in memory. When we have enough records\\n244:  * batched, we will insert them into a database. If we allowed offsets to auto commit as in the previous example, records\\n245:  * would be considered consumed after they were returned to the user in {@link #poll(Duration) poll}. It would then be\\n246:  * possible\\n247:  * for our process to fail after batching the records, but before they had been inserted into the database.\\n248:  * <p>\\n249:  * To avoid this, we will manually commit the offsets only after the corresponding records have been inserted into the\\n250:  * database. This gives us exact control of when a record is considered consumed. This raises the opposite possibility:\\n251:  * the process could fail in the interval after the insert into the database but before the commit (even though this\\n252:  * would likely just be a few milliseconds, it is a possibility). In this case the process that took over consumption\\n253:  * would consume from last committed offset and would repeat the insert of the last batch of data. Used in this way\\n254:  * Kafka provides what is often called \\\"at-least-once\\\" delivery guarantees, as each record will likely be delivered one\\n255:  * time but in failure cases could be duplicated.\\n256:  * <p>\\n257:  * <b>Note: Using automatic offset commits can also give you \\\"at-least-once\\\" delivery, but the requirement is that\\n258:  * you must consume all data returned from each call to {@link #poll(Duration)} before any subsequent calls, or before\\n259:  * {@link #close() closing} the consumer. If you fail to do either of these, it is possible for the committed offset\\n260:  * to get ahead of the consumed position, which results in missing records. The advantage of using manual offset\\n261:  * control is that you have direct control over when a record is considered \\\"consumed.\\\"</b>\\n262:  * <p>\\n263:  * The above example uses {@link #commitSync() commitSync} to mark all received records as committed. In some cases\\n264:  * you may wish to have even finer control over which records have been committed by specifying an offset explicitly.\\n265:  * In the example below we commit offset after we finish handling the records in each partition.\\n266:  * <p>\\n267:  * <pre>\\n268:  * {@code\\n269:  *     try {\\n270:  *         while(running) {\\n271:  *             ConsumerRecords<String, String> records = consumer.poll(Duration.ofMillis(Long.MAX_VALUE));\\n272:  *             for (TopicPartition partition : records.partitions()) {\\n273:  *                 List<ConsumerRecord<String, String>> partitionRecords = records.records(partition);\\n274:  *                 for (ConsumerRecord<String, String> record : partitionRecords) {\\n275:  *                     System.out.println(record.offset() + \\\": \\\" + record.value());\\n276:  *                 }\\n277:  *                 consumer.commitSync(Collections.singletonMap(partition, records.nextOffsets().get(partition)));\\n278:  *             }\\n279:  *         }\\n280:  *     } finally {\\n281:  *       consumer.close();\\n282:  *     }\\n283:  * }</pre>\\n284:  *\\n285:  * <b>Note: The committed offset should always be the offset of the next message that your application will read.</b>\\n286:  * Thus, when calling {@link #commitSync(Map) commitSync(offsets)} you should use {@code nextRecordToBeProcessed.offset()}\\n287:  * or if {@link ConsumerRecords} is exhausted already {@link ConsumerRecords#nextOffsets()} instead.\\n288:  * You should also add the leader epoch as commit metadata, which can be obtained from\\n289:  * {@link ConsumerRecord#leaderEpoch()} or {@link ConsumerRecords#nextOffsets()}.\\n290:  *\\n291:  * <h4><a name=\\\"manualassignment\\\">Manual Partition Assignment</a></h4>\\n292:  *\\n293:  * In the previous examples, we subscribed to the topics we were interested in and let Kafka dynamically assign a\\n294:  * fair share of the partitions for those topics based on the active consumers in the group. However, in\\n295:  * some cases you may need finer control over the specific partitions that are assigned. For example:\\n296:  * <p>\\n297:  * <ul>\\n298:  * <li>If the process is maintaining some kind of local state associated with that partition (like a\\n299:  * local on-disk key-value store), then it should only get records for the partition it is maintaining on disk.\\n300:  * <li>If the process itself is highly available and will be restarted if it fails (perhaps using a\\n301:  * cluster management framework like YARN, Mesos, or AWS facilities, or as part of a stream processing framework). In\\n302:  * this case there is no need for Kafka to detect the failure and reassign the partition since the consuming process\\n303:  * will be restarted on another machine.\\n304:  * </ul>\\n305:  * <p>\\n306:  * To use this mode, instead of subscribing to the topic using {@link #subscribe(Collection) subscribe}, you just call\\n307:  * {@link #assign(Collection)} with the full list of partitions that you want to consume.\\n308:  *\\n309:  * <pre>\\n310:  * {@code\\n311:  *     String topic = \\\"foo\\\";\\n312:  *     TopicPartition partition0 = new TopicPartition(topic, 0);\\n313:  *     TopicPartition partition1 = new TopicPartition(topic, 1);\\n314:  *     consumer.assign(Arrays.asList(partition0, partition1));\\n315:  * }</pre>\\n316:  *\\n317:  * Once assigned, you can call {@link #poll(Duration) poll} in a loop, just as in the preceding examples to consume\\n318:  * records. The group that the consumer specifies is still used for committing offsets, but now the set of partitions\\n319:  * will only change with another call to {@link #assign(Collection) assign}. Manual partition assignment does\\n320:  * not use group coordination, so consumer failures will not cause assigned partitions to be rebalanced. Each consumer\\n321:  * acts independently even if it shares a groupId with another consumer. To avoid offset commit conflicts, you should\\n322:  * usually ensure that the groupId is unique for each consumer instance.\\n323:  * <p>\\n324:  * Note that it isn't possible to mix manual partition assignment (i.e. using {@link #assign(Collection) assign})\\n325:  * with dynamic partition assignment through topic subscription (i.e. using {@link #subscribe(Collection) subscribe}).\\n326:  *\\n327:  * <h4><a name=\\\"rebalancecallback\\\">Storing Offsets Outside Kafka</h4>\\n328:  *\\n329:  * The consumer application need not use Kafka's built-in offset storage, it can store offsets in a store of its own\\n330:  * choosing. The primary use case for this is allowing the application to store both the offset and the results of the\\n331:  * consumption in the same system in a way that both the results and offsets are stored atomically. This is not always\\n332:  * possible, but when it is it will make the consumption fully atomic and give \\\"exactly once\\\" semantics that are\\n333:  * stronger than the default \\\"at-least once\\\" semantics you get with Kafka's offset commit functionality.\\n334:  * <p>\\n335:  * Here are a couple of examples of this type of usage:\\n336:  * <ul>\\n337:  * <li>If the results of the consumption are being stored in a relational database, storing the offset in the database\\n338:  * as well can allow committing both the results and offset in a single transaction. Thus either the transaction will\\n339:  * succeed and the offset will be updated based on what was consumed or the result will not be stored and the offset\\n340:  * won't be updated.\\n341:  * <li>If the results are being stored in a local store it may be possible to store the offset there as well. For\\n342:  * example a search index could be built by subscribing to a particular partition and storing both the offset and the\\n343:  * indexed data together. If this is done in a way that is atomic, it is often possible to have it be the case that even\\n344:  * if a crash occurs that causes unsync'd data to be lost, whatever is left has the corresponding offset stored as well.\\n345:  * This means that in this case the indexing process that comes back having lost recent updates just resumes indexing\\n346:  * from what it has ensuring that no updates are lost.\\n347:  * </ul>\\n348:  * <p>\\n349:  * Each record comes with its own offset, so to manage your own offset you just need to do the following:\\n350:  *\\n351:  * <ul>\\n352:  * <li>Configure <code>enable.auto.commit=false</code>\\n353:  * <li>Use the offset provided with each {@link ConsumerRecord} to save your position.\\n354:  * <li>On restart restore the position of the consumer using {@link #seek(TopicPartition, long)}.\\n355:  * </ul>\\n356:  *\\n357:  * <p>\\n358:  * This type of usage is simplest when the partition assignment is also done manually (this would be likely in the\\n359:  * search index use case described above). If the partition assignment is done automatically special care is\\n360:  * needed to handle the case where partition assignments change. This can be done by providing a\\n361:  * {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener} instance in the call to {@link #subscribe(Collection, ConsumerRebalanceListener)}\\n362:  * and {@link #subscribe(Pattern, ConsumerRebalanceListener)}.\\n363:  * For example, when partitions are taken from a consumer the consumer will want to commit its offset for those partitions by\\n364:  * implementing {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener#onPartitionsRevoked(Collection)}. When partitions are assigned to a\\n365:  * consumer, the consumer will want to look up the offset for those new partitions and correctly initialize the consumer\\n366:  * to that position by implementing {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener#onPartitionsAssigned(Collection)}.\\n367:  * <p>\\n368:  * Another common use for {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener} is to flush any caches the application maintains for\\n369:  * partitions that are moved elsewhere.\\n370:  *\\n371:  * <h4>Controlling The Consumer's Position</h4>\\n372:  *\\n373:  * In most use cases the consumer will simply consume records from beginning to end, periodically committing its\\n374:  * position (either automatically or manually). However Kafka allows the consumer to manually control its position,\\n375:  * moving forward or backwards in a partition at will. This means a consumer can re-consume older records, or skip to\\n376:  * the most recent records without actually consuming the intermediate records.\\n377:  * <p>\\n378:  * There are several instances where manually controlling the consumer's position can be useful.\\n379:  * <p>\\n380:  * One case is for time-sensitive record processing it may make sense for a consumer that falls far enough behind to not\\n381:  * attempt to catch up processing all records, but rather just skip to the most recent records.\\n382:  * <p>\\n383:  * Another use case is for a system that maintains local state as described in the previous section. In such a system\\n384:  * the consumer will want to initialize its position on start-up to whatever is contained in the local store. Likewise\\n385:  * if the local state is destroyed (say because the disk is lost) the state may be recreated on a new machine by\\n386:  * re-consuming all the data and recreating the state (assuming that Kafka is retaining sufficient history).\\n387:  * <p>\\n388:  * Kafka allows specifying the position using {@link #seek(TopicPartition, long)} to specify the new position. Special\\n389:  * methods for seeking to the earliest and latest offset the server maintains are also available (\\n390:  * {@link #seekToBeginning(Collection)} and {@link #seekToEnd(Collection)} respectively).\\n391:  *\\n392:  * <h4>Consumption Flow Control</h4>\\n393:  *\\n394:  * If a consumer is assigned multiple partitions to fetch data from, it will try to consume from all of them at the same time,\\n395:  * effectively giving these partitions the same priority for consumption. However in some cases consumers may want to\\n396:  * first focus on fetching from some subset of the assigned partitions at full speed, and only start fetching other partitions\\n397:  * when these partitions have few or no data to consume.\\n398:  *\\n399:  * <p>\\n400:  * One of such cases is stream processing, where processor fetches from two topics and performs the join on these two streams.\\n401: \",\"url\":\"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-benchmarks/kafka--e678b4b@47ac12bd6042edf23f1dbca3d789db945d62802d/-/blob/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/clients/consumer/KafkaConsumer.java%3FL200-400\"}"
27. mcp__sourcegraph__sg_read_file @ -
Input
{
  "endLine": 600,
  "path": "clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/clients/consumer/KafkaConsumer.java",
  "repo": "github.com/sg-benchmarks/kafka--e678b4b",
  "startLine": 400
}
Output
"{\"content\":\"400:  * One of such cases is stream processing, where processor fetches from two topics and performs the join on these two streams.\\n401:  * When one of the topics is long lagging behind the other, the processor would like to pause fetching from the ahead topic\\n402:  * in order to get the lagging stream to catch up. Another example is bootstrapping upon consumer starting up where there are\\n403:  * a lot of history data to catch up, the applications usually want to get the latest data on some of the topics before consider\\n404:  * fetching other topics.\\n405:  *\\n406:  * <p>\\n407:  * Kafka supports dynamic controlling of consumption flows by using {@link #pause(Collection)} and {@link #resume(Collection)}\\n408:  * to pause the consumption on the specified assigned partitions and resume the consumption\\n409:  * on the specified paused partitions respectively in the future {@link #poll(Duration)} calls.\\n410:  *\\n411:  * <h3>Reading Transactional Messages</h3>\\n412:  *\\n413:  * <p>\\n414:  * Transactions were introduced in Kafka 0.11.0 wherein applications can write to multiple topics and partitions atomically.\\n415:  * In order for this to work, consumers reading from these partitions should be configured to only read committed data.\\n416:  * This can be achieved by setting the {@code isolation.level=read_committed} in the consumer's configuration.\\n417:  *\\n418:  * <p>\\n419:  * In <code>read_committed</code> mode, the consumer will read only those transactional messages which have been\\n420:  * successfully committed. It will continue to read non-transactional messages as before. There is no client-side\\n421:  * buffering in <code>read_committed</code> mode. Instead, the end offset of a partition for a <code>read_committed</code>\\n422:  * consumer would be the offset of the first message in the partition belonging to an open transaction. This offset\\n423:  * is known as the 'Last Stable Offset'(LSO).</p>\\n424:  *\\n425:  * <p>\\n426:  * A {@code read_committed} consumer will only read up to the LSO and filter out any transactional\\n427:  * messages which have been aborted. The LSO also affects the behavior of {@link #seekToEnd(Collection)} and\\n428:  * {@link #endOffsets(Collection)} for {@code read_committed} consumers, details of which are in each method's documentation.\\n429:  * Finally, the fetch lag metrics are also adjusted to be relative to the LSO for {@code read_committed} consumers.\\n430:  *\\n431:  * <p>\\n432:  * Partitions with transactional messages will include commit or abort markers which indicate the result of a transaction.\\n433:  * There markers are not returned to applications, yet have an offset in the log. As a result, applications reading from\\n434:  * topics with transactional messages will see gaps in the consumed offsets. These missing messages would be the transaction\\n435:  * markers, and they are filtered out for consumers in both isolation levels. Additionally, applications using\\n436:  * {@code read_committed} consumers may also see gaps due to aborted transactions, since those messages would not\\n437:  * be returned by the consumer and yet would have valid offsets.\\n438:  *\\n439:  * <h3><a name=\\\"multithreaded\\\">Multi-threaded Processing</a></h3>\\n440:  *\\n441:  * The Kafka consumer is NOT thread-safe. It is the responsibility of the user to ensure that multi-threaded access\\n442:  * is properly synchronized. Un-synchronized access will result in {@link ConcurrentModificationException}.\\n443:  *\\n444:  * <p>\\n445:  * The only exception to this rule is {@link #wakeup()}, which can safely be used from an external thread to\\n446:  * interrupt an active operation. In this case, a {@link org.apache.kafka.common.errors.WakeupException} will be\\n447:  * thrown from the thread blocking on the operation. This can be used to shutdown the consumer from another thread.\\n448:  * The following snippet shows the typical pattern:\\n449:  *\\n450:  * <pre>\\n451:  * {@code\\n452:  * public class KafkaConsumerRunner implements Runnable {\\n453:  *     private final AtomicBoolean closed = new AtomicBoolean(false);\\n454:  *     private final KafkaConsumer consumer;\\n455:  *\\n456:  *     public KafkaConsumerRunner(KafkaConsumer consumer) {\\n457:  *       this.consumer = consumer;\\n458:  *     }\\n459:  *\\n460:  *     @Override\\n461:  *     public void run() {\\n462:  *         try {\\n463:  *             consumer.subscribe(Arrays.asList(\\\"topic\\\"));\\n464:  *             while (!closed.get()) {\\n465:  *                 ConsumerRecords records = consumer.poll(Duration.ofMillis(10000));\\n466:  *                 // Handle new records\\n467:  *             }\\n468:  *         } catch (WakeupException e) {\\n469:  *             // Ignore exception if closing\\n470:  *             if (!closed.get()) throw e;\\n471:  *         } finally {\\n472:  *             consumer.close();\\n473:  *         }\\n474:  *     }\\n475:  *\\n476:  *     // Shutdown hook which can be called from a separate thread\\n477:  *     public void shutdown() {\\n478:  *         closed.set(true);\\n479:  *         consumer.wakeup();\\n480:  *     }\\n481:  * }\\n482:  * }</pre>\\n483:  *\\n484:  * Then in a separate thread, the consumer can be shutdown by setting the closed flag and waking up the consumer.\\n485:  *\\n486:  * <p>\\n487:  * <pre>\\n488:  * {@code\\n489:  *     closed.set(true);\\n490:  *     consumer.wakeup();\\n491:  * }</pre>\\n492:  *\\n493:  * <p>\\n494:  * Note that while it is possible to use thread interrupts instead of {@link #wakeup()} to abort a blocking operation\\n495:  * (in which case, {@link InterruptException} will be raised), we discourage their use since they may cause a clean\\n496:  * shutdown of the consumer to be aborted. Interrupts are mainly supported for those cases where using {@link #wakeup()}\\n497:  * is impossible, e.g. when a consumer thread is managed by code that is unaware of the Kafka client.\\n498:  *\\n499:  * <p>\\n500:  * We have intentionally avoided implementing a particular threading model for processing. This leaves several\\n501:  * options for implementing multi-threaded processing of records.\\n502:  *\\n503:  * <h4>1. One Consumer Per Thread</h4>\\n504:  *\\n505:  * A simple option is to give each thread its own consumer instance. Here are the pros and cons of this approach:\\n506:  * <ul>\\n507:  * <li><b>PRO</b>: It is the easiest to implement\\n508:  * <li><b>PRO</b>: It is often the fastest as no inter-thread co-ordination is needed\\n509:  * <li><b>PRO</b>: It makes in-order processing on a per-partition basis very easy to implement (each thread just\\n510:  * processes messages in the order it receives them).\\n511:  * <li><b>CON</b>: More consumers means more TCP connections to the cluster (one per thread). In general Kafka handles\\n512:  * connections very efficiently so this is generally a small cost.\\n513:  * <li><b>CON</b>: Multiple consumers means more requests being sent to the server and slightly less batching of data\\n514:  * which can cause some drop in I/O throughput.\\n515:  * <li><b>CON</b>: The number of total threads across all processes will be limited by the total number of partitions.\\n516:  * </ul>\\n517:  *\\n518:  * <h4>2. Decouple Consumption and Processing</h4>\\n519:  *\\n520:  * Another alternative is to have one or more consumer threads that do all data consumption and hands off\\n521:  * {@link ConsumerRecords} instances to a blocking queue consumed by a pool of processor threads that actually handle\\n522:  * the record processing.\\n523:  *\\n524:  * This option likewise has pros and cons:\\n525:  * <ul>\\n526:  * <li><b>PRO</b>: This option allows independently scaling the number of consumers and processors. This makes it\\n527:  * possible to have a single consumer that feeds many processor threads, avoiding any limitation on partitions.\\n528:  * <li><b>CON</b>: Guaranteeing order across the processors requires particular care as the threads will execute\\n529:  * independently an earlier chunk of data may actually be processed after a later chunk of data just due to the luck of\\n530:  * thread execution timing. For processing that has no ordering requirements this is not a problem.\\n531:  * <li><b>CON</b>: Manually committing the position becomes harder as it requires that all threads co-ordinate to ensure\\n532:  * that processing is complete for that partition.\\n533:  * </ul>\\n534:  *\\n535:  * There are many possible variations on this approach. For example each processor thread can have its own queue, and\\n536:  * the consumer threads can hash into these queues using the TopicPartition to ensure in-order consumption and simplify\\n537:  * commit.\\n538:  */\\n539: public class KafkaConsumer<K, V> implements Consumer<K, V> {\\n540: \\n541:     private static final ConsumerDelegateCreator CREATOR = new ConsumerDelegateCreator();\\n542: \\n543:     private final ConsumerDelegate<K, V> delegate;\\n544: \\n545:     /**\\n546:      * A consumer is instantiated by providing a set of key-value pairs as configuration. Valid configuration strings\\n547:      * are documented <a href=\\\"http://kafka.apache.org/documentation.html#consumerconfigs\\\" >here</a>. Values can be\\n548:      * either strings or objects of the appropriate type (for example a numeric configuration would accept either the\\n549:      * string \\\"42\\\" or the integer 42).\\n550:      * <p>\\n551:      * Valid configuration strings are documented at {@link ConsumerConfig}.\\n552:      * <p>\\n553:      * Note: after creating a {@code KafkaConsumer} you must always {@link #close()} it to avoid resource leaks.\\n554:      *\\n555:      * @param configs The consumer configs\\n556:      */\\n557:     public KafkaConsumer(Map<String, Object> configs) {\\n558:         this(configs, null, null);\\n559:     }\\n560: \\n561:     /**\\n562:      * A consumer is instantiated by providing a {@link java.util.Properties} object as configuration.\\n563:      * <p>\\n564:      * Valid configuration strings are documented at {@link ConsumerConfig}.\\n565:      * <p>\\n566:      * Note: after creating a {@code KafkaConsumer} you must always {@link #close()} it to avoid resource leaks.\\n567:      *\\n568:      * @param properties The consumer configuration properties\\n569:      */\\n570:     public KafkaConsumer(Properties properties) {\\n571:         this(properties, null, null);\\n572:     }\\n573: \\n574:     /**\\n575:      * A consumer is instantiated by providing a {@link java.util.Properties} object as configuration, and a\\n576:      * key and a value {@link Deserializer}.\\n577:      * <p>\\n578:      * Valid configuration strings are documented at {@link ConsumerConfig}.\\n579:      * <p>\\n580:      * Note: after creating a {@code KafkaConsumer} you must always {@link #close()} it to avoid resource leaks.\\n581:      *\\n582:      * @param properties The consumer configuration properties\\n583:      * @param keyDeserializer The deserializer for key that implements {@link Deserializer}. The configure() method\\n584:      *            won't be called in the consumer when the deserializer is passed in directly.\\n585:      * @param valueDeserializer The deserializer for value that implements {@link Deserializer}. The configure() method\\n586:      *            won't be called in the consumer when the deserializer is passed in directly.\\n587:      */\\n588:     public KafkaConsumer(Properties properties,\\n589:                          Deserializer<K> keyDeserializer,\\n590:                          Deserializer<V> valueDeserializer) {\\n591:         this(propsToMap(properties), keyDeserializer, valueDeserializer);\\n592:     }\\n593: \\n594:     /**\\n595:      * A consumer is instantiated by providing a set of key-value pairs as configuration, and a key and a value {@link Deserializer}.\\n596:      * <p>\\n597:      * Valid configuration strings are documented at {@link ConsumerConfig}.\\n598:      * <p>\\n599:      * Note: after creating a {@code KafkaConsumer} you must always {@link #close()} it to avoid resource leaks.\\n600:      *\\n601: \",\"url\":\"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-benchmarks/kafka--e678b4b@47ac12bd6042edf23f1dbca3d789db945d62802d/-/blob/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/clients/consumer/KafkaConsumer.java%3FL400-600\"}"

"{\"content\":\"600:      *\\n601:      * @param configs The consumer configs\\n602:      * @param keyDeserializer The deserializer for key that implements {@link Deserializer}. The configure() method\\n603:      *            won't be called in the consumer when the deserializer is passed in directly.\\n604:      * @param valueDeserializer The deserializer for value that implements {@link Deserializer}. The configure() method\\n605:      *            won't be called in the consumer when the deserializer is passed in directly.\\n606:      */\\n607:     public KafkaConsumer(Map<String, Object> configs,\\n608:                          Deserializer<K> keyDeserializer,\\n609:                          Deserializer<V> valueDeserializer) {\\n610:         this(new ConsumerConfig(ConsumerConfig.appendDeserializerToConfig(configs, keyDeserializer, valueDeserializer)),\\n611:                 keyDeserializer, valueDeserializer);\\n612:     }\\n613: \\n614:     KafkaConsumer(ConsumerConfig config, Deserializer<K> keyDeserializer, Deserializer<V> valueDeserializer) {\\n615:         delegate = CREATOR.create(config, keyDeserializer, valueDeserializer);\\n616:     }\\n617: \\n618:     KafkaConsumer(LogContext logContext,\\n619:                   Time time,\\n620:                   ConsumerConfig config,\\n621:                   Deserializer<K> keyDeserializer,\\n622:                   Deserializer<V> valueDeserializer,\\n623:                   KafkaClient client,\\n624:                   SubscriptionState subscriptions,\\n625:                   ConsumerMetadata metadata,\\n626:                   List<ConsumerPartitionAssignor> assignors) {\\n627:         delegate = CREATOR.create(\\n628:             logContext,\\n629:             time,\\n630:             config,\\n631:             keyDeserializer,\\n632:             valueDeserializer,\\n633:             client,\\n634:             subscriptions,\\n635:             metadata,\\n636:             assignors\\n637:         );\\n638:     }\\n639: \\n640:     /**\\n641:      * Get the set of partitions currently assigned to this consumer. If subscription happened by directly assigning\\n642:      * partitions using {@link #assign(Collection)} then this will simply return the same partitions that\\n643:      * were assigned. If topic subscription was used, then this will give the set of topic partitions currently assigned\\n644:      * to the consumer (which may be none if the assignment hasn't happened yet, or the partitions are in the\\n645:      * process of getting reassigned).\\n646:      * @return The set of partitions currently assigned to this consumer\\n647:      */\\n648:     public Set<TopicPartition> assignment() {\\n649:         return delegate.assignment();\\n650:     }\\n651: \\n652:     /**\\n653:      * Get the current subscription. Will return the same topics used in the most recent call to\\n654:      * {@link #subscribe(Collection, ConsumerRebalanceListener)}, or an empty set if no such call has been made.\\n655:      * @return The set of topics currently subscribed to\\n656:      */\\n657:     public Set<String> subscription() {\\n658:         return delegate.subscription();\\n659:     }\\n660: \\n661:     /**\\n662:      * Subscribe to the given list of topics to get dynamically\\n663:      * assigned partitions. <b>Topic subscriptions are not incremental. This list will replace the current\\n664:      * assignment (if there is one).</b> Note that it is not possible to combine topic subscription with group management\\n665:      * with manual partition assignment through {@link #assign(Collection)}.\\n666:      *\\n667:      * If the given list of topics is empty, it is treated the same as {@link #unsubscribe()}.\\n668:      *\\n669:      * <p>\\n670:      * As part of group management, the group coordinator will keep track of the list of consumers that belong to a particular\\n671:      * group and will trigger a rebalance operation if any one of the following events are triggered:\\n672:      * <ul>\\n673:      * <li>Number of partitions change for any of the subscribed topics\\n674:      * <li>A subscribed topic is created or deleted\\n675:      * <li>An existing member of the consumer group is shutdown or fails\\n676:      * <li>A new member is added to the consumer group\\n677:      * </ul>\\n678:      * <p>\\n679:      * When any of these events are triggered, the provided listener will be invoked in this way:\\n680:      * <ul>\\n681:      *     <li>{@link ConsumerRebalanceListener#onPartitionsRevoked(Collection)} will be invoked with the partitions to revoke, before re-assigning those partitions to another consumer.</li>\\n682:      *     <li>{@link ConsumerRebalanceListener#onPartitionsAssigned(Collection)} will be invoked when the rebalance completes (even if no new partitions are assigned to the consumer)</li>\\n683:      * </ul>\\n684:      * Note that rebalances will only occur during an active call to {@link #poll(Duration)}, so callbacks will\\n685:      * also only be invoked during that time.\\n686:      *\\n687:      * The provided listener will immediately override any listener set in a previous call to subscribe.\\n688:      * It is guaranteed, however, that the partitions revoked/assigned through this interface are from topics\\n689:      * subscribed in this call. See {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener} for more details.\\n690:      *\\n691:      * @param topics The list of topics to subscribe to\\n692:      * @param listener Non-null listener instance to get notifications on partition assignment/revocation for the\\n693:      *                 subscribed topics\\n694:      * @throws IllegalArgumentException If topics is null or contains null or empty elements, or if listener is null\\n695:      * @throws IllegalStateException If {@code subscribe()} is called previously with pattern, or assign is called\\n696:      *                               previously (without a subsequent call to {@link #unsubscribe()}), or if not\\n697:      *                               configured at-least one partition assignment strategy\\n698:      */\\n699:     @Override\\n700:     public void subscribe(Collection<String> topics, ConsumerRebalanceListener listener) {\\n701:         delegate.subscribe(topics, listener);\\n702:     }\\n703: \\n704:     /**\\n705:      * Subscribe to the given list of topics to get dynamically assigned partitions.\\n706:      * <b>Topic subscriptions are not incremental. This list will replace the current\\n707:      * assignment (if there is one).</b> It is not possible to combine topic subscription with group management\\n708:      * with manual partition assignment through {@link #assign(Collection)}.\\n709:      *\\n710:      * If the given list of topics is empty, it is treated the same as {@link #unsubscribe()}.\\n711:      *\\n712:      * <p>\\n713:      * This is a short-hand for {@link #subscribe(Collection, ConsumerRebalanceListener)}, which\\n714:      * uses a no-op listener. If you need the ability to seek to particular offsets, you should prefer\\n715:      * {@link #subscribe(Collection, ConsumerRebalanceListener)}, since group rebalances will cause partition offsets\\n716:      * to be reset. You should also provide your own listener if you are doing your own offset\\n717:      * management since the listener gives you an opportunity to commit offsets before a rebalance finishes.\\n718:      *\\n719:      * @param topics The list of topics to subscribe to\\n720:      * @throws IllegalArgumentException If topics is null or contains null or empty elements\\n721:      * @throws IllegalStateException If {@code subscribe()} is called previously with pattern, or assign is called\\n722:      *                               previously (without a subsequent call to {@link #unsubscribe()}), or if not\\n723:      *                               configured at-least one partition assignment strategy\\n724:      */\\n725:     @Override\\n726:     public void subscribe(Collection<String> topics) {\\n727:         delegate.subscribe(topics);\\n728:     }\\n729: \\n730:     /**\\n731:      * Subscribe to all topics matching specified pattern to get dynamically assigned partitions.\\n732:      * The pattern matching will be done periodically against all topics existing at the time of check.\\n733:      * This can be controlled through the {@code metadata.max.age.ms} configuration: by lowering\\n734:      * the max metadata age, the consumer will refresh metadata more often and check for matching topics.\\n735:      * <p>\\n736:      * See {@link #subscribe(Collection, ConsumerRebalanceListener)} for details on the\\n737:      * use of the {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener}. Generally rebalances are triggered when there\\n738:      * is a change to the topics matching the provided pattern and when consumer group membership changes.\\n739:      * Group rebalances only take place during an active call to {@link #poll(Duration)}.\\n740:      *\\n741:      * @param pattern Pattern to subscribe to\\n742:      * @param listener Non-null listener instance to get notifications on partition assignment/revocation for the\\n743:      *                 subscribed topics\\n744:      * @throws IllegalArgumentException If pattern or listener is null\\n745:      * @throws IllegalStateException If {@code subscribe()} is called previously with topics, or assign is called\\n746:      *                               previously (without a subsequent call to {@link #unsubscribe()}), or if not\\n747:      *                               configured at-least one partition assignment strategy\\n748:      */\\n749:     @Override\\n750:     public void subscribe(Pattern pattern, ConsumerRebalanceListener listener) {\\n751:         delegate.subscribe(pattern, listener);\\n752:     }\\n753: \\n754:     /**\\n755:      * Subscribe to all topics matching specified pattern to get dynamically assigned partitions.\\n756:      * The pattern matching will be done periodically against topics existing at the time of check.\\n757:      * <p>\\n758:      * This is a short-hand for {@link #subscribe(Pattern, ConsumerRebalanceListener)}, which\\n759:      * uses a no-op listener. If you need the ability to seek to particular offsets, you should prefer\\n760:      * {@link #subscribe(Pattern, ConsumerRebalanceListener)}, since group rebalances will cause partition offsets\\n761:      * to be reset. You should also provide your own listener if you are doing your own offset\\n762:      * management since the listener gives you an opportunity to commit offsets before a rebalance finishes.\\n763:      *\\n764:      * @param pattern Pattern to subscribe to\\n765:      * @throws IllegalArgumentException If pattern is null\\n766:      * @throws IllegalStateException If {@code subscribe()} is called previously with topics, or assign is called\\n767:      *                               previously (without a subsequent call to {@link #unsubscribe()}), or if not\\n768:      *                               configured at-least one partition assignment strategy\\n769:      */\\n770:     @Override\\n771:     public void subscribe(Pattern pattern) {\\n772:         delegate.subscribe(pattern);\\n773:     }\\n774: \\n775:     /**\\n776:      * Subscribe to all topics matching the specified pattern, to get dynamically assigned partitions.\\n777:      * The pattern matching will be done periodically against all topics. This is only supported under the\\n778:      * CONSUMER group protocol (see {@link ConsumerConfig#GROUP_PROTOCOL_CONFIG}).\\n779:      * <p>\\n780:      * If the provided pattern is not compatible with Google RE2/J, an {@link InvalidRegularExpression} will be\\n781:      * eventually thrown on a call to {@link #poll(Duration)} following this call to subscribe.\\n782:      * <p>\\n783:      * See {@link #subscribe(Collection, ConsumerRebalanceListener)} for details on the\\n784:      * use of the {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener}. Generally, rebalances are triggered when there\\n785:      * is a change to the topics matching the provided pattern and when consumer group membership changes.\\n786:      * Group rebalances only take place during an active call to {@link #poll(Duration)}.\\n787:      *\\n788:      * @param pattern  Pattern to subscribe to, that must be compatible with Google RE2/J.\\n789:      * @param listener Non-null listener instance to get notifications on partition assignment/revocation for the\\n790:      *                 subscribed topics.\\n791:      * @throws IllegalArgumentException If pattern is null or empty, or if the listener is null.\\n792:      * @throws IllegalStateException    If {@code subscribe()} is called previously with topics, or assign is called\\n793:      *                                  previously (without a subsequent call to {@link #unsubscribe()}).\\n794:      */\\n795:     @Override\\n796:     public void subscribe(SubscriptionPattern pattern, ConsumerRebalanceListener listener) {\\n797:         delegate.subscribe(pattern, listener);\\n798:     }\\n799: \\n800:     /**\\n801: \",\"url\":\"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-benchmarks/kafka--e678b4b@47ac12bd6042edf23f1dbca3d789db945d62802d/-/blob/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/clients/consumer/KafkaConsumer.java%3FL600-800\"}"

Output
"{\"content\":\"800:     /**\\n801:      * Subscribe to all topics matching the specified pattern, to get dynamically assigned partitions.\\n802:      * The pattern matching will be done periodically against topics. This is only supported under the\\n803:      * CONSUMER group protocol (see {@link ConsumerConfig#GROUP_PROTOCOL_CONFIG})\\n804:      * <p>\\n805:      * If the provided pattern is not compatible with Google RE2/J, an {@link InvalidRegularExpression} will be\\n806:      * eventually thrown on a call to {@link #poll(Duration)} following this call to subscribe.\\n807:      * <p>\\n808:      * This is a short-hand for {@link #subscribe(Pattern, ConsumerRebalanceListener)}, which\\n809:      * uses a no-op listener. If you need the ability to seek to particular offsets, you should prefer\\n810:      * {@link #subscribe(Pattern, ConsumerRebalanceListener)}, since group rebalances will cause partition offsets\\n811:      * to be reset. You should also provide your own listener if you are doing your own offset\\n812:      * management since the listener gives you an opportunity to commit offsets before a rebalance finishes.\\n813:      *\\n814:      * @param pattern Pattern to subscribe to, that must be compatible with Google RE2/J.\\n815:      * @throws IllegalArgumentException If pattern is null or empty.\\n816:      * @throws IllegalStateException    If {@code subscribe()} is called previously with topics, or assign is called\\n817:      *                                  previously (without a subsequent call to {@link #unsubscribe()}).\\n818:      */\\n819:     @Override\\n820:     public void subscribe(SubscriptionPattern pattern) {\\n821:         delegate.subscribe(pattern);\\n822:     }\\n823: \\n824:     /**\\n825:      * Unsubscribe from topics currently subscribed with {@link #subscribe(Collection)} or {@link #subscribe(Pattern)}.\\n826:      * This also clears any partitions directly assigned through {@link #assign(Collection)}.\\n827:      *\\n828:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.KafkaException for any other unrecoverable errors (e.g. rebalance callback errors)\\n829:      */\\n830:     public void unsubscribe() {\\n831:         delegate.unsubscribe();\\n832:     }\\n833: \\n834:     /**\\n835:      * Manually assign a list of partitions to this consumer. This interface does not allow for incremental assignment\\n836:      * and will replace the previous assignment (if there is one).\\n837:      * <p>\\n838:      * If the given list of topic partitions is empty, it is treated the same as {@link #unsubscribe()}.\\n839:      * <p>\\n840:      * Manual topic assignment through this method does not use the consumer's group management\\n841:      * functionality. As such, there will be no rebalance operation triggered when group membership or cluster and topic\\n842:      * metadata change. Note that it is not possible to use both manual partition assignment with {@link #assign(Collection)}\\n843:      * and group assignment with {@link #subscribe(Collection, ConsumerRebalanceListener)}.\\n844:      * <p>\\n845:      * If auto-commit is enabled, an async commit (based on the old assignment) will be triggered before the new\\n846:      * assignment replaces the old one.\\n847:      *\\n848:      * @param partitions The list of partitions to assign this consumer\\n849:      * @throws IllegalArgumentException If partitions is null or contains null or empty topics\\n850:      * @throws IllegalStateException If {@code subscribe()} is called previously with topics or pattern\\n851:      *                               (without a subsequent call to {@link #unsubscribe()})\\n852:      */\\n853:     @Override\\n854:     public void assign(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions) {\\n855:         delegate.assign(partitions);\\n856:     }\\n857: \\n858:     /**\\n859:      * Fetch data for the topics or partitions specified using one of the subscribe/assign APIs. It is an error to not have\\n860:      * subscribed to any topics or partitions before polling for data.\\n861:      * <p>\\n862:      * On each poll, consumer will try to use the last consumed offset as the starting offset and fetch sequentially. The last\\n863:      * consumed offset can be manually set through {@link #seek(TopicPartition, long)} or automatically set as the last committed\\n864:      * offset for the subscribed list of partitions\\n865:      *\\n866:      * <p>\\n867:      * This method returns immediately if there are records available or if the position advances past control records\\n868:      * or aborted transactions when isolation.level=read_committed.\\n869:      * Otherwise, it will await the passed timeout. If the timeout expires, an empty record set will be returned.\\n870:      * Note that this method may block beyond the timeout in order to execute custom\\n871:      * {@link ConsumerRebalanceListener} callbacks.\\n872:      *\\n873:      *\\n874:      * @param timeout The maximum time to block (must not be greater than {@link Long#MAX_VALUE} milliseconds)\\n875:      *\\n876:      * @return map of topic to records since the last fetch for the subscribed list of topics and partitions\\n877:      *\\n878:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.clients.consumer.InvalidOffsetException if the offset for a partition or set of\\n879:      *             partitions is undefined or out of range and no offset reset policy has been configured\\n880:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.WakeupException if {@link #wakeup()} is called before or while this\\n881:      *             function is called\\n882:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.InterruptException if the calling thread is interrupted before or while\\n883:      *             this function is called\\n884:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.AuthenticationException if authentication fails. See the exception for more details\\n885:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.AuthorizationException if caller lacks Read access to any of the subscribed\\n886:      *             topics or to the configured groupId. See the exception for more details\\n887:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.KafkaException for any other unrecoverable errors (e.g. invalid groupId or\\n888:      *             session timeout, errors deserializing key/value pairs, your rebalance callback thrown exceptions,\\n889:      *             or any new error cases in future versions)\\n890:      * @throws java.lang.IllegalArgumentException if the timeout value is negative\\n891:      * @throws java.lang.IllegalStateException if the consumer is not subscribed to any topics or manually assigned any\\n892:      *             partitions to consume from\\n893:      * @throws java.lang.ArithmeticException if the timeout is greater than {@link Long#MAX_VALUE} milliseconds.\\n894:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.InvalidTopicException if the current subscription contains any invalid\\n895:      *             topic (per {@link org.apache.kafka.common.internals.Topic#validate(String)})\\n896:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.UnsupportedVersionException if the consumer attempts to fetch stable offsets\\n897:      *             when the broker doesn't support this feature. Also, if the consumer attempts to subscribe to a\\n898:      *             SubscriptionPattern via {@link #subscribe(SubscriptionPattern)} or\\n899:      *             {@link #subscribe(SubscriptionPattern, ConsumerRebalanceListener)} and the broker doesn't\\n900:      *             support this feature.\\n901:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.FencedInstanceIdException if this consumer instance gets fenced by broker.\\n902:      */\\n903:     @Override\\n904:     public ConsumerRecords<K, V> poll(final Duration timeout) {\\n905:         return delegate.poll(timeout);\\n906:     }\\n907: \\n908:     /**\\n909:      * Commit offsets returned on the last {@link #poll(Duration) poll()} for all the subscribed list of topics and\\n910:      * partitions.\\n911:      * <p>\\n912:      * This commits offsets only to Kafka. The offsets committed using this API will be used on the first fetch after\\n913:      * every rebalance and also on startup. As such, if you need to store offsets in anything other than Kafka, this API\\n914:      * should not be used.\\n915:      * <p>\\n916:      * This is a synchronous commit and will block until either the commit succeeds, an unrecoverable error is\\n917:      * encountered (in which case it is thrown to the caller), or the timeout specified by {@code default.api.timeout.ms} expires\\n918:      * (in which case a {@link org.apache.kafka.common.errors.TimeoutException} is thrown to the caller).\\n919:      * <p>\\n920:      * Note that asynchronous offset commits sent previously with the {@link #commitAsync(OffsetCommitCallback)}\\n921:      * (or similar) are guaranteed to have their callbacks invoked prior to completion of this method,\\n922:      * but only when the consumer is using the consumer group protocol.\\n923:      *\\n924:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.clients.consumer.CommitFailedException if the commit failed and cannot be retried.\\n925:      *             This fatal error can only occur if you are using automatic group management with {@link #subscribe(Collection)},\\n926:      *             or if there is an active group with the same <code>group.id</code> which is using group management. In such cases,\\n927:      *             when you are trying to commit to partitions that are no longer assigned to this consumer because the\\n928:      *             consumer is for example no longer part of the group this exception would be thrown.\\n929:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.RebalanceInProgressException if the consumer instance is in the middle of a rebalance\\n930:      *            so it is not yet determined which partitions would be assigned to the consumer. In such cases you can first\\n931:      *            complete the rebalance by calling {@link #poll(Duration)} and commit can be reconsidered afterwards.\\n932:      *            NOTE when you reconsider committing after the rebalance, the assigned partitions may have changed,\\n933:      *            and also for those partitions that are still assigned their fetch positions may have changed too\\n934:      *            if more records are returned from the {@link #poll(Duration)} call.\\n935:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.WakeupException if {@link #wakeup()} is called before or while this\\n936:      *             function is called\\n937:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.InterruptException if the calling thread is interrupted before or while\\n938:      *             this function is called\\n939:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.AuthenticationException if authentication fails. See the exception for more details\\n940:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.AuthorizationException if not authorized to the topic or to the\\n941:      *             configured groupId. See the exception for more details\\n942:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.KafkaException for any other unrecoverable errors (e.g. if offset metadata\\n943:      *             is too large or if the topic does not exist).\\n944:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.TimeoutException if the timeout specified by {@code default.api.timeout.ms} expires\\n945:      *            before successful completion of the offset commit\\n946:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.FencedInstanceIdException if this consumer is using the classic group protocol\\n947:      *            and this instance gets fenced by broker.\\n948:      */\\n949:     @Override\\n950:     public void commitSync() {\\n951:         delegate.commitSync();\\n952:     }\\n953: \\n954:     /**\\n955:      * Commit offsets returned on the last {@link #poll(Duration) poll()} for all the subscribed list of topics and\\n956:      * partitions.\\n957:      * <p>\\n958:      * This commits offsets only to Kafka. The offsets committed using this API will be used on the first fetch after\\n959:      * every rebalance and also on startup. As such, if you need to store offsets in anything other than Kafka, this API\\n960:      * should not be used.\\n961:      * <p>\\n962:      * This is a synchronous commit and will block until either the commit succeeds, an unrecoverable error is\\n963:      * encountered (in which case it is thrown to the caller), or the passed timeout expires.\\n964:      * <p>\\n965:      * Note that asynchronous offset commits sent previously with the {@link #commitAsync(OffsetCommitCallback)}\\n966:      * (or similar) are guaranteed to have their callbacks invoked prior to completion of this method,\\n967:      * but only when the consumer is using the consumer group protocol.\\n968:      *\\n969:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.clients.consumer.CommitFailedException if the commit failed and cannot be retried.\\n970:      *             This can only occur if you are using automatic group management with {@link #subscribe(Collection)},\\n971:      *             or if there is an active group with the same <code>group.id</code> which is using group management. In such cases,\\n972:      *             when you are trying to commit to partitions that are no longer assigned to this consumer because the\\n973:      *             consumer is for example no longer part of the group this exception would be thrown.\\n974:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.RebalanceInProgressException if the consumer instance is in the middle of a rebalance\\n975:      *            so it is not yet determined which partitions would be assigned to the consumer. In such cases you can first\\n976:      *            complete the rebalance by calling {@link #poll(Duration)} and commit can be reconsidered afterwards.\\n977:      *            NOTE when you reconsider committing after the rebalance, the assigned partitions may have changed,\\n978:      *            and also for those partitions that are still assigned their fetch positions may have changed too\\n979:      *            if more records are returned from the {@link #poll(Duration)} call.\\n980:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.WakeupException if {@link #wakeup()} is called before or while this\\n981:      *             function is called\\n982:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.InterruptException if the calling thread is interrupted before or while\\n983:      *             this function is called\\n984:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.AuthenticationException if authentication fails. See the exception for more details\\n985:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.AuthorizationException if not authorized to the topic or to the\\n986:      *             configured groupId. See the exception for more details\\n987:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.KafkaException for any other unrecoverable errors (e.g. if offset metadata\\n988:      *             is too large or if the topic does not exist).\\n989:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.TimeoutException if the timeout expires before successful completion\\n990:      *            of the offset commit\\n991:      * @throws org.apache.kafka.common.errors.FencedInstanceIdException if this consumer is using the classic group protocol\\n992:      *            and this instance gets fenced by broker.\\n993:      */\\n994:     @Override\\n995:     public void commitSync(Duration timeout) {\\n996:         delegate.commitSync(timeout);\\n997:     }\\n998: \\n999:     /**\\n1000:      * Commit the specified offsets for the specified list of topics and partitions.\\n1001: \",\"url\":\"https://sourcegraph.sourcegraph.com/github.com/sg-benchmarks/kafka--e678b4b@47ac12bd6042edf23f1dbca3d789db945d62802d/-/blob/clients/src/main/java/org/apache/kafka/clients/consumer/KafkaConsumer.java%3FL800-1000\"}"
